coredata.dc:title: Laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 nanoparticles and fluorescence of carbon dots as dual-mode signals for the specific detection of arsenic(V) in environmental water samples

coredata.dc:description: The detrimental growth of water pollutants such as heavy metals has become a life-threatening problem in the modern era. Challenges remain in the development of rapid and accurate methods for detecting pentavalent arsenic [As(V)] in environmental water. The octahedral Mn3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) did not display excellent laccase-mimicking catalytic activity, whereas the adsorbed As(V) on the surface significantly enhanced the catalytic activity. Meanwhile, the quinone imine generated from the substrates 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DP) and 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AAP) catalyzed by octahedral Mn3O4 NPs further quenched the carbon dots fluorescence. Thus, it is possible to establish a fast and accurate dual-mode sensor for detecting As(V). The developed dual-mode method of As(V) detection has good sensitivity and selectivity. The limit of detection for As(V) in colorimetric mode is 6.96 μg·L−1, whereas in the fluorescent mode, it is as low as 2.56 μg·L−1. Moreover, the detection data obtained by the dual-mode method can be validated by each other, thereby ensuring the dependability of the sensing system. The constructed dual-mode method with merits of sensitivity, speed and accuracy can offer a powerful tool for As(V) detection in environmental water. Furthermore, the application of laccase-mimicking activity in dual-mode detection provides new strategies for other environmental hazard detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and CDs, and validation of As(V)-enhanced laccase-mimicking activity and quenching fluorescence by the catalytic product. (A) SEM images of pure octahedral Mn3O4 NPs. (B) SEM images of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs with As(V). (C) XPS spectra of Mn3O4 NPs with or without As(V); (D) Mn2p XPS spectra of pure Mn3O4 NPs; (E) Mn2p XPS spectra of Mn3O4 NPs with adsorbed As(V); (F) change in the Mn2+ content of Mn3O4 NPs; (G) O 1s XPS spectra of pure Mn3O4 NPs; (H) O 1s XPS spectra of Mn3O4 NPs with adsorbed As(V); (I) TEM image of CDs; Insert: Particle size distribution map of CDs; (J) HRTEM images of CDs; (K) validation of As(V)-enhanced laccase-mimicking activity of the Mn3O4 NPs; Sample 1: Mn3O4 NPs; Sample 2: Mn3O4 NPs + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 3: Mn3O4 NPs + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + 400 μg·L−1 As(V); Sample 4: 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; (L) the impact of quinone imine on CDs fluorescence; (A) Sample 1: CDs; Sample 2: CDs + Mn3O4 NPs; Sample 3: 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + Sample 2; Sample 4: 2,4-DP + 4-AAP + CDs; Sample 5: 400 μg·L−1 As(V) + Sample 3; Sample 6: 400 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs.

Investigation on the quenching mechanism of fluorescence. (A) Normalized excitation and emission spectra of CDs and normalized absorption spectra of quinone imines. (B) Stern-Volmer linear equations for F0/F and different As(V) quantities at 297 K, 310 K, and 318 K; the error bar represents the standard deviation of three repeated detections. (C) Comparison of fluorescence lifetimes between CDs and CDs + quinone imines.
Schematic of the dual-mode method for As (V) detection.

Feasibility of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode for As(V) detection. (A) Feasibility of colorimetric mode for As(V) detection. Sample 1: CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP; Sample 2: 20 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP +2,4-DP; Sample 3: 100 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP; Sample 4: 400 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP. (B) Feasibility of the fluorescent mode for As(V) detection. Sample 1: CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP; Sample 2: 20 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP; Sample 3: 100 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP; Sample 4: 400 μg·L−1 As(V) + CDs + Mn3O4 NPs + 4-AAP + 2,4-DP.

Investigation of the mechanism of As(V)-enhanced laccase-mimicking activity. (A) ESR spectra of the changes in semiquinone radicals produced by Mn3O4 NPs before and after interaction with As(V). (B) The influence of tert-butanol on the laccase-mimicking activity of Mn3O4 NPs in different solutions. (C) The influence of p-benzoquinone on the laccase-mimicking activity of Mn3O4 NPs in different solutions; the error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated detections. (D) Changes in peak current in different reaction solutions; Sample 1: 2,4-DP; Sample 2: Mn3O4 NPs + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 3: 100 μg·L−1 As(V) + Mn3O4 NPs + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP; Sample 4: 400 μg·L−1 As(V) + Mn3O4 NPs + 2,4-DP + 4-AAP.

Sensitivity of the colorimetric and fluorescence channels for As(V) detection. (A) Color changes after adding As(V) solutions with different concentrations and the calibration curve for ∆A and As(V) content. (B) Changes in fluorescence intensity of sensing solutions after adding different concentrations of As(V) and calibration curve for ∆F and As(V) contents. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated detections.

Selectivity of the colorimetric and fluorescence channels for As(V) detection. (A) Color changes and ∆A value after adding different analyte solutions. (B) Fluorescence changes and ∆F value after adding different analyte solutions. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated detections.
Analysis of As(V) in actual environmental water samples.

head.ce:title: Laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 nanoparticles and fluorescence of carbon dots as dual-mode signals for the specific detection of arsenic(V) in environmental water samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detrimental growth of water pollutants such as heavy metals has become a life-threatening problem in the modern era. Challenges remain in the development of rapid and accurate methods for detecting pentavalent arsenic [As(V)] in environmental water. The octahedral Mn3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) did not display excellent laccase-mimicking catalytic activity, whereas the adsorbed As(V) on the surface significantly enhanced the catalytic activity. Meanwhile, the quinone imine generated from the substrates 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DP) and 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AAP) catalyzed by octahedral Mn3O4 NPs further quenched the carbon dots fluorescence. Thus, it is possible to establish a fast and accurate dual-mode sensor for detecting As(V). The developed dual-mode method of As(V) detection has good sensitivity and selectivity. The limit of detection for As(V) in colorimetric mode is 6.96 μg·L−1, whereas in the fluorescent mode, it is as low as 2.56 μg·L−1. Moreover, the detection data obtained by the dual-mode method can be validated by each other, thereby ensuring the dependability of the sensing system. The constructed dual-mode method with merits of sensitivity, speed and accuracy can offer a powerful tool for As(V) detection in environmental water. Furthermore, the application of laccase-mimicking activity in dual-mode detection provides new strategies for other environmental hazard detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Arsenic (As) is a ubiquitous heavy metal that exists in organic and inorganic forms in water, soil, and food (Haldar et al., 2020; Thakkar et al., 2021). Arsenic is insoluble in water and is almost nontoxic; however, inorganic compounds trivalent arsenic [As(III)] and pentavalent arsenic [As(V)], are highly toxic (Rivera-Nunez et al., 2012). Exposure to arsenic over a long period can cause many health problems, including cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes and high blood pressure (Rahimi et al., 2021; Rani et al., 2020). Arsenic is currently classified as a human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer and the US Centers for Disease Control, and the maximum limit for arsenic in water was set at 10 μg·L−1 by the World Health Organization (WHO) (Ding et al., 2023; Laketic et al., 2013). Therefore, determining arsenic in water is imperative. Currently, the main arsenic detection methods are atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) (Guo et al., 2017), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Caruso et al., 2001), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Le et al., 2021) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with the above methods (Lan Anh et al., 2011). Although these techniques are capable of quantitatively detecting arsenic, the cost of the assay, sample pretreatment, real-time detection, and portability limit the breadth of their application. Thus, developing economical, portable and rapid methods for arsenic quantification is crucial.

Electrochemical signal detection (Sonkoue et al., 2018), the surface enhancement by Raman scattering (SERS) method (Yang et al., 2019), colorimetric method (Choodum et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2013) and fluorescence method (Wang et al., 2023) have been widely used for arsenic quantitative detection in water. Nevertheless, the limitations of single optical detection methods, such as susceptibility to false alarms and unreliable data, render them unsuitable for the detection of hazards in outdoor scenarios. It would be beneficial to consider the construction of a dual-mode optical sensor for biological and environmental detection. According to previous reports, dual-channel detection mainly involves colorimetric/SERS (Sharma et al., 2022), colorimetric/fluorescence method (Gupta et al., 2016), and photoelectrochemical methods (Wang et al., 2022b; Xu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019), which can compensate for the optical shortcomings of single-mode methods in practical applications. In comparison with colorimetric/SERS, the fluorescence/colorimetric method has more diversified detection modes owing to the simultaneous changes in the dual signals. Thus, it meets the sensitivity and visualization requirements of the detection process. However, most of the sensing materials applied for colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode methods are modified fluorescent materials and gold nanoparticles. Such materials are subjected to a complicated preparation process and are easily affected by surrounding environmental factors, resulting in inaccurate detection results. Therefore, it is important to search for stable fluorescence and colorimetric sensing materials for the development of stable and reliable colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode analysis of arsenic.

Carbon dots (CDs) are organic-inorganic hybrid materials with superior fluorescence properties over conventional semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent dyes. They are highly regarded for their low toxicity, ease of dupability and excellent chemical stability (Xia et al., 2019). The doping of nonmetallic elements in CDs will compensate for some of their shortcomings. For instance, it may alter their optical properties and electronic structure and also improve and increase the yield quantum of CDs as well as active sites (Lin et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2016). In comparison to CDs that are not doped, nitrogen-doped CDs exhibit a greater fluorescence quantum efficiency and wider range of applications (Sahu et al., 2022). Nitrogen-doped CDs have been successfully employed as fluorescence signals in dual-mode analysis methods with fluorescence colorimetric, while they are often matched with gold nanoparticles as dual-signal sensing materials (Tan et al., 2024). Manganese oxide, as a typical transition metal oxide, has attracted widespread attention owing to its broad application prospects in catalysis, energy storage, sensing, adsorption, and magnetic applications (Bai et al., 2015; Hao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020). In addition, owing to the superior oxidase-like activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, single-signal colorimetric sensors based on octahedral Mn3O4 NPs have been successfully developed and applied to detect various substances (Wang et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022). There are research reports that spinel-like Mn3O4 NPs have good laccase-mimicking activity (Wang et al., 2022a), and a colorimetric sensor array was successfully constructed that can effectively identify and distinguish phenol and its derivatives in tap water samples. However, there is a lack of reports on the laccase-like activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, and there is little research on the application of Mn3O4 NPs with octahedral shape as colorimetric signal carrier for dual-signal detection.

Herein, a novel dual-mode with colorimetric and fluorescence strategy for the rapid detection of As(V) in environmental water samples was developed, and the CDs and octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were chosen as a dual-mode sensing carrier. In this study, the change of color from the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs enhanced by As(V) was employed on the colorimetric channel, whereas the change of fluorescence caused by quenching CDs fluorescence with the catalytic product was applied for fluorescence channel. Convenient and accurate detection of As(V) residues in water can be achieved by the correspondence between the changes in colorimetric and fluorescence signals and As(V) concentration. Hence, it is anticipated that such a dual-mode detection method, offering the advantages of rapidity, stability and accuracy, will be employed for the large-scale qualitative and quantitative analysis of As(V) in water samples.

Potassium permanganate (KMn3O4) was purchased from Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 4-Aminoantipyrine (4-AAP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DP), citric acid (CA), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), O-phenylenediamine, tert-butanol, p-benzoquinone, N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N′-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer, polyethylene glycol (PEG-200), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and other standard solutions were all obtained from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). As(III) and As(V) standard solutions were purchased from the Northern Weiye Metrology Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents used were of analytical grade. The pH of the HEPES buffer was adjusted by NaOH. The solvent for p-benzoquinone was DMF, whereas all other reagents, such as the standard arsenic solution, were dissolved or diluted with ultrapure water.

A magnetic stirrer with a constant temperature was employed to prepare octahedral Mn3O4 NPs. The UV/visible absorption spectra and absorbance were recorded using a microplate spectrophotometer (Thermoelectric) from the United States. The elements and functional groups of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Quantera II SXM, PHI, USA). The morphological characteristics of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi). The electron microscopy images and particle sizes of CDs were measured using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 TEM, Japan). An electron spin resonance energy spectrometry (BRUKER EMXPLUS) was used to obtain electron spin resonance (ESR) energy spectrometer signal data. The fluorescence intensity was measured using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted using the CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument, USA). Ultrapure water was prepared for the entire experiment using the Millipore ultrapure water mechanism. The contents of As(V) in actual water samples were measured by ICP-MS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, ICAP Q) to validate the application of the dual-mode method.

The preparation of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs was based on existing literature (Wang et al., 2021). The KMnO4 (0.0525 g) was added to 15 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 15 mL of PEG-200 and a thorough reaction for 30 min. The mixture was subsequently reacted at 120 °C for 8 h. Once the reaction had sufficiently cooled, the reaction product was filtered and washed three times with C2H5OH and ultrapure water. Finally, the brown precipitate collected by centrifugation was placed in a vacuum dryer at 60 °C for 12 h.

The addition of 0.108 g of O-phenylenediamine and 0.192 g of CA to 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by sonication for 10 min, was recommended (Hao et al., 2022). The solution was transferred to the reaction vessel and stored at 120 °C for 6 h, after which it was cooled to 25 °C. Afterward, the discolored product (500 MW) was dialyzed for 12 h. Finally, a filter membrane (0.22 μm) was utilized to filter the above solution, thereby obtaining a solution of CDs, which was further stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of semiquinone radicals and oxygen radicals
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the relationship between As(V)-enhanced laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and semiquinone radicals, the octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were thoroughly mixed with As(V) solution and reacted at 30 °C for 60 min. It was followed by the addition of 450 μL of HEPES buffer solution, thorough mixing and the sampling of the mixture using a capillary tube. The mixture was then detected under visible light via ESR energy spectrometry. Tert-butanol is a scavenger of hydroxyl radical (·OH), whereas p-benzoquinone is a scavenger of superoxide anion (O2 −). Therefore, tert-butanol and p-benzoquinone were employed to monitor the levels of OH and O2 − catalyzed by the octahedral Mn3O4 NPs. First, 10 μL As(V) solution and Mn3O4 NPs (10 μL) were mixed and allowed to react at 40 °C for 60 min. Subsequently, a solution containing different concentrations of tert-butanol (10 μL), 450 μL of HEPES buffer, 10 μL of 2,4-DP, and 10 μL of 4-AAP should be combined to create a 500 μL detection solution. Then, the absorbance value should be measured at 510 nm. The exploration experiment of O2 − is analogous to that of OH, with the sole distinction being that tert-butanol in the system is substituted with p-benzoquinone.

The working electrode should be a glass carbon electrode, the comparative electrode should be a platinum wire, and the reference electrode should be Ag/AgCl. The measurement mode was primarily configured to utilize a starting potential of 0 V, an ending potential of 1.0 V, and a scanning speed of 50 mV·s−1. To assess the response current values of 2,4-DP, 4-AAP, octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, and As(V) solutions in HEPES buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) under such mode.

As(V) solutions with different concentrations (10 μL) and the octahedral Mn3O4 NPs (10 μL) were added into a centrifuge tube (1.5 mL), after which the mixture was mixed thoroughly and incubated at a temperature of 40 °C for the specified period of 60 min. Then, 450 μL of HEPES buffer, 10 μL of 2,4-DP, 4-AAP, and 10 μL of CDs were mixed in the centrifuge tube to obtain 500 μL of assay solution. Afterward, the ultrapure water (10 μL) replaced the As(V) solution as a control. Finally, the absorbance value (510 nm) of the test sample (A) and the control sample (A0) was obtained. In this case, the change in the absorbance value (∆A = A − A0) was a colorimetric signal. Concurrently, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system was observed, and the fluorescence values (432 nm) of the test sample (F) and control sample (F0) were measured to calculate the change of fluorescence value (∆F = F0 − F), which was then used as the fluorescence sensing signal response.

Tap water, lake water and river water were chosen as typical test samples and reference was made to previous literature for processing such samples (Xia et al., 2024). The tap water samples were obtained from the laboratory of Guizhou University, whereas the lake and river water were obtained from Guizhou University and Huaxi River Water in Guiyang. Subsequently, different concentrations of As(V) solutions were added to the above samples, and the filtrate was then collected using a needle filter (0.22 μm) after 20 min of standing. Once the collection of filtrates from the three samples was complete, the actual samples were tested and the recovery studies were conducted.

The shape of Mn3O4 NPs before and after the addition of As(V) was analyzed by SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1A, Mn3O4 NPs exhibited an octahedral shape, with a diameter of approximately 100 nm, which was consistent with previous reports (Li et al., 2011). Following the addition of As(V), the morphology of Mn3O4 NPs transformed from a smooth to a rough surface, indicating that As (V) was successfully adsorbed on the surface of Mn3O4 NPs and reacted (Fig. 1B) (Babaeivelni et al., 2014). Moreover, the elemental and valence transitions on the surface of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were investigated using XPS. As shown in Fig. 1C, the pure octahedral Mn3O4 NPs comprised only two elements, Mn and O, except for C element, which was at 299.08 eV. The presence of element C can be attributed to residual polyethylene glycol, which was not subjected to washing completely during the preparation of the material. Notably, the appearance of As 3d in the plots proved that As(V) had successfully adsorbed and reacted with octahedral Mn3O4 NPs.

To confirm the adsorption and reaction between As(V) and octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, the XPS spectrograms of Mn 2p1/2 region and Mn 2p3/2 region in Mn 2p were exhibited as shown in Fig. 1D. Peaks in the Mn 2p1/2 region were identified at 652.95 and 653.65 eV, unequivocally corresponding to the Mn3+ and Mn4+ states, respectively. Peaks in the Mn 2p3/2 region were identified at 640.55, 641.0, and 642.15 eV, corresponding to Mn2+, Mn3+, and Mn4+, respectively (Birkner et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2019; Thankalekshmi and Rastogi, 2014). The values in question were found to be in concordance with the reported binding energy values for Mn in Mn3O4 NPs. Fig. 1E presents the peak intensities of the Mn 2p region of Mn3O4 NPs following its reaction with As(V). It can be observed that the peak intensities of Mn4+ and Mn3+ were diminished, and the Mn2+ peak intensity was augmented. Such conversion of Mn3+ and Mn4+ to Mn2+ in Mn3O4 NPs after reaction with As(V) was also further illustrated in Fig. 1F. The XPS mapping of O 1s presented three peaks in Mn3O4 NPs. The XPS spectrum of Mn3O4 NPs alone (Fig. 1G) revealed the presence of three peaks at 530.12 eV, 531.15 eV and 533.0 eV, which were attributed to M-O, M-OH and H-O-H, respectively (Birkner et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2019; Han et al., 2006). Fig. 1H presents the O 1 s profile of Mn3O4 NPs following its reaction with As(V). A slight increase in the peak areas of H-O-H and M-O was indicated, further corroborating the successful adsorption and interaction of As(V) with Mn3O4 NPs.

As illustrated in Fig. 1I, the shape of CDs was spherical and evenly distributed on the copper grid. Fig. 1J presents a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of the CDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.17 nm, corresponding to the graphite carbon (100) plane. These results indicate that the prepared CDs exhibit a highly crystalline carbon structure (Tang et al., 2014). A statistical analysis of the particle size distribution of CDs revealed an average particle size of approximately 2.93 nm. The above results indicate that the CDs have been successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between sensing materials

ce:section.ce:para: Previous reports have indicated that the laccase-catalyzed colorimetric reaction is owing to the oxidation of substrate 2,4-DP by mimetics with laccase activity to generate semiquinone radicals, which combine with substrate 4-AAP to generate a red product quinone imine and appear a distinct absorption peak at 510 nm (Huang et al., 2023; Xia et al., 2024). To determine the As(V)-enhanced laccase-mimicking catalytic activity, chromogenic substrates (2,4-DP and 4-AAP) were employed to measure the changes in the color of various solutions and the absorption spectra in the 300–700 nm range. As presented in Fig. 1K, the solution did not exhibit any discernible characteristic absorption peaks within the 300–700 nm range when octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were only present, and the solution appeared colorless (Sample 1). Upon the addition of 2,4-DP and 4-AAP to octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, the solution still exhibited almost colorless, and no discernible characteristic absorption peak was observed within 300–700 nm (Sample 2). Once the As(V) solution was added to Sample 2, the solution exhibited a pronounced red color and a distinctive absorption peak at 510 nm (Sample 3). Furthermore, the mixed solutions of 2,4-DP and 4-AAP were colorless, and exhibited no absorption peaks from 300 nm to 700 nm (Sample 4). The aforementioned experiments demonstrate that As(V) enhances the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, further catalyzes the generation of the red product quinone imine from 2,4-DP and 4-AAP, and induces significant color changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of quinone imine on the fluorescence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 1L, a pure CDs solution can emit bright fluorescence, with a relatively strong emission wavelength at 432 nm (Sample 1). Upon the addition of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs to the CDs solution, a slight change was observed in the emission spectrum and fluorescence signal at 432 nm (Sample 2), which can be attributed to the background color interference of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs (Fig. S1). Following the addition of a mixed solution comprising 2,4-DP and 4-AAP to the mixture of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and CDs solution, no discernible alteration was observed in the emission spectrum and fluorescence signal of the solution at 432 nm (Sample 3). The effect of a mixed solution of 2,4-DP and 4-AAP on the fluorescence of CDs was also examined. The results demonstrated that the emission spectrum and fluorescence signal at 432 nm did not show significant changes compared with the pure CDs solution (Sample 4). Once the As(V) solution was mixed into the solution (2,4-DP, 4-AAP, Mn3O4 NPs and CDs solution), the fluorescence signal of CDs at 432 nm significantly decreased (Sample 5). Moreover, the individual As(V) solution did not affect the emission intensity of CDs (Sample 6). As shown in Fig. S2, the differences in fluorescence between pure CDs solution (Sample 1), a CDs mixed solution with octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and As(V) (Sample 2), a CDs mixed solution with octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, As(V) and 2,4-DP (Sample 3), and a CDs mixed solution with octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, As(V) and 4-AAP (Sample 4) were also examined. The phenomenon demonstrated that the fluorescence signal of CDs decreased only when octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, As(V), 2,4-DP, and 4-AAP solutions were simultaneously present. Therefore, evidently, the As(V) enhanced the laccase-mimicking activity of Mn3O4 NPs, and the 2,4-DP and 4-AAP will be catalyzed to form the red product quinone imine. Furthermore, the quinone imine further quenches the fluorescence of CDs, thus, the As(V) content correlated with the degree of fluorescence change.

To determine the quenching mechanism of the color product quinone imine on CDs, spectroscopy tests of fluorescence emission and UV–visible absorption data were finished. As demonstrated in Fig. 2A, the absorption spectrum of quinone imine overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs, indicating that quinone imine could quench CDs fluorescence by the internal filtration effect (IFE). Furthermore, the impact of temperature on the quenching constant was also examined. The Stern-Volmer equation, asserts that F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q] was employed to investigate the quenching constants at temperatures of 297 K, 310 K, and 318 K, where Ksv and [Q] are the Stern-Volmer quenching constants and concentrations of As(V). As can be seen from Fig. 2B, Ksv increased with temperature, which can be attributed to the dynamic quenching effect (DQE). In addition, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is 9.99934 ± 0.01106 ns, whereas the average lifetime of CDs with quinone imine is 7.49711 ± 0.00613 ns, showing a significant change (Fig. 2C) (Aswathy et al., 2020). In this case, it has been demonstrated that quinone imine induces fluorescence quenching of CDs through IFE and DQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a dual-mode method for As(V) detection

ce:section.ce:para: A dual-mode method for As(V) detection has been established that relies on the quenching of CDs fluorescence by quinone imine. The principle of dual-mode method is illustrated in Fig. 3 . Following the adsorption and interaction of As(V) with octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, the morphology and valence state of Mn3O4 NPs undergo transformation. This resulted in enhanced laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and the production of greater quantities of red products, (e.g., quinone imine), which was further used to quench CDs fluorescence through IFE and DQE. In the absence of an As(V) solution within the sensing system, the sensing solution exhibits a colorless and high fluorescence background signal. Conversely, in the presence of As(V) solution, the sensing solution presents a red and low fluorescence background signal. Evidently, the color and fluorescence changes of the sensing solution are positively correlated with the concentration of As(V). Therefore, a dual-mode method for As(V) detection was developed using fluorescence intensity of CDs and the UV absorption of quinone imines as output signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of dual-mode method for As(V) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the analysis of the constructed dual-mode method, as shown in Fig. 4A, changes in the color and absorption spectra (400–700 nm) of various detection solutions were collected and recorded. The individual octahedral Mn3O4 NPs exhibited weak laccase-mimicking activity and were almost unable to catalyze the production of quinone imines by 2,4-DP and 4-AAP, resulting in a colorless detection solution and no significant absorption peak (510 nm) generated (Sample 1). When As(V) was mixed into the above detection solution, the absorption peak of sensing solutions (Samples 2–4) became progressively stronger. The color transition occurred, evolving from a clear, colorless state to a distinct red hue, in correlation with the rising concentration of As(V). In addition, fluorescence emission spectra and the changes in fluorescence intensity of different solutions were measured (Fig. 4B). The mixed solution of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, 2,4-DP, 4-AAP and CDs showed a distinct characteristic fluorescence peak at 432 nm and emitted a bright blue color under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation (Sample 1). The fluorescence emission peak gradually decreased if the As(V) content continuously increased, and the fluorescence signal of the sensing solution also gradually became weaker (Samples 2–4). The above results indicate that As(V) enhances the laccase-mimicking activity of Mn3O4 NPs, thereby increasing the generation of quinone imines and further weakening the fluorescence of CDs. Hence, both of colorimetric and fluorescent signals can be applied for As(V) detection.

As presented in Fig. 5A, the ESR spectra of the detection solution before and after the addition of As(V) were measured to explore the interaction between the laccase-mimicking activity of Mn3O4 NPs and semiquinone radicals. If octahedral Mn3O4 NPs were only present in the detection solution, the ESR signal was relatively weak, confirming that octahedral Mn3O4 NPs can oxidize 2,4-DP to generate trace amounts of semiquinone radicals. In comparison, the ESR signal of the solution significantly increased with the addition of As(V). It was proved that As(V) can accelerate or increase the formation of semiquinone radicals. According to previous studies (Wang et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022), the catalytic oxidation of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs is closely related to the production of reactive oxygen species (OH/O2 −). The efficacy of tert-butanol as free radical scavenger was investigated by examining the changes in ·OH content before and after the addition of As(V) to the detection solution. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the OH content remains unchanged after the addition of As(V) to the detection solution, indicating that there is no necessary correlation between OH and the laccase activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs. Concurrently, p-benzoquinone was employed as a capturing agent to monitor the alterations in the quantity of O2 − prior to and following the interaction between octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and As(V). As shown in Fig. 5C, the content of O2 − in the presence of As(V) was much higher than that of O2 − generated by 2,4-DP catalyzed by individual octahedral Mn3O4 NPs at the same concentration of p-benzoquinone. Thus, As(V) enhances the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs by accelerating or increasing the formation of semiquinone radicals O2 −.

Furthermore, the mechanism of the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs enhanced with As(V) was elucidated through CV. As Fig. 5D demonstrated, 2,4-DP exhibited a peak current of 10.26 μA in the HEPES buffer system (Sample 1). Following the addition of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs and the 4-AAP to 2,4-DP solution, the peak current of the mixture increased from 10.26 μA to 10.64 μA (Sample 2). As the As(V) concentration increased, the peak current of the mixture demonstrated a further obvious increase to 11.46 μA (Sample 3) and 12.82 μA (Sample 4). In addition, Fig. S3 presents the current values of individual octahedral Mn3O4 NPs (Sample 1), 4-AAP (Sample 2), and As(V) (Sample 3) solutions. The above results indicate that octahedral Mn3O4 NPs adsorbed with As(V) enhances the electron transfer between Mn3O4 NPs and 2,4-DP. Thus, the generation of free radicals increased, and the reaction solution exhibited a distinct red. Thus, it can be inferred that As(V) can increase the electron transfer from octahedral Mn3O4 NPs to 2,4-DP, producing more semiquinone radicals and O2 −, thereby enhancing the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs.

To improve the detection performance of the constructed dual-mode method, HEPES buffer, substrates, Mn3O4 NPs, incubation temperature and incubation time were investigated (see the Support Information). The following experimental conditions were found to give the best results: (a) a HEPES buffer with a quantity of 10 mM and a pH value of 7.0 (Fig. S4A and S4B), (b) 10 mg·mL−1 of 2,4-DP and 5 mg·mL−1 4-AAP (Fig. S5A and S5B), (c) 25 μg·mL−1 of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs (Fig. S6A and S6B), and (d) incubation at 40 °C for 60 min (Fig. S7A and S7B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of dual-mode method for As(V) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensitivity of the constructed dual-mode method, As(V) was quantitatively measured under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6A, the sensing solution gradually turned red and the absorption peak (510 nm) continued to increase as the As(V) concentration gradually increased. When the content of As(V) exceeded 400 μg·L−1, the colorimetric signal (ΔA) exhibited almost constant, indicating that the adsorption and interaction between As(V) and octahedral Mn3O4 NPs had reached saturation. In this case, the relationship between As(V) concentration and ΔA showed a linear relationship in the range of 10–150 μg·L−1 (y = 0.003× + 0.010). The limit of detection (LOD) of the colorimetric channel for As(V) was calculated as 6.96 μg·L−1 according to 3σ/slope (Gao et al., 2024, 2023). Concurrently, As(V) quantification can be attained through fluorescence channels. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of initial sensing system is relatively high. The presence of a solution containing 10–2000 μg·L−1 As(V) in the sensing system decrease in the fluorescence signal, and the fluorescence spectrum showed a decreasing trend in the range of 400–600 nm. When the As(V) concentration exceeded 600 μg·L−1, the fluorescence remained unchanged. The As(V) concentration is related to the fluorescence at the range of 10–300 μg·L−1, with a linear relationship (y = 0.600× + 56.745) between the two variables. The 3σ/slope calculation yielded a lower LOD value of 2.56 μg·L−1 for As(V) in the fluorescence channel.

The LOD of the constructed dual-mode detection method is lower than the maximum residual value of As(V) in water set by WHO (10 μg·L−1), which indicates that the detection method has good sensitivity for As(V) detection. Fig. S8 illustrates the correlation between As(V) concentration, as determined by fluorescence and colorimetric analysis. The data indicates a linear correlation (R2 = 0.9918) between the fluorescence and colorimetric mode, suggesting that the dual-mode channel detection data can be corroborated, thereby enhancing the reliability of the As(V) sensing system. A comparison of the detection performance of the constructed dual-mode method with other methods, such as electrochemistry, fluorescence, and colorimetry was shown in Table S1. Although the constructed dual-mode method has lower sensitivity than other electrochemistry and fluorescence methods, it can meet the minimum limits (10 μg·L−1) for arsenic in water set by WHO. In addition, the dual-mode detection method does not require any modification and exhibits a good linear range. Moreover, the constructed dual-mode detection method can output signals through fluorescence and colorimetric modes, enhancing the reliability and accuracy of the detection results compared with single signal detection methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of dual-mode method for As(V) detection
ce:section.ce:para: A series of tests were conducted to assess the absorbance values and fluorescence responses of various ion sensing platforms. The research ions were As(III) (Sample 2), Fe(II) (Sample 3), K(I) (Sample 4), Fe(II) (Sample 5), Cr(VI) (Sample 6), Hg(II) (Sample 7), Zn(II) (Sample 8), Ag(I) (Sample 9), Mg(II) (Sample 10), Ca(II) (Sample 11), Na(II) (Sample 12), and Co(II) (Sample 13). As illustrated in Fig. 7A-B, the detection solution only appeared red and the fluorescence was significantly quenched in the presence of As(V). It is worth noting that As(III) has no effect on colorimetric and fluorescent channels. and it can be speculated that As(V) will be more easily adsorbed on the surface of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs than As(III) in the constructed dual-mode detection system (Panda et al., 2019). The above conclusion indicates that the developed detection strategy exhibits excellent anti-interference ability and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of As(V) in actual environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed dual-mode method was evaluated with tap water, river water, and lake water as actual samples (Table 1 ). The average detection recovery rate of As(V) was 89.88–98.41 % on the colorimetric channel, with an average relative standard deviation of 1.14–5.71 %. In the fluorescence channel, the average detection recovery rate of As(V) was found to be 86.40–99.03 %, with an RSD of 1.17–4.53 %. These results indicate that the constructed detection method exhibits a high level of accuracy and practical applicability, and can provide a sensitive and rapid new detection strategy for As(V) detection in environmental water samples. Moreover, ICP-MS was applied to measure the same water samples to validate the accuracy and feasibility of the dual-mode method. The results demonstrated the efficacy of the constructed dual-mode method for actual sample detection, indicating that the method can be serve as an effective alternative for the rapid detection of arsenic (V) residues in water.

Herein, the As(V) would directly influence the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs, and the catalytic products in the catalytic system would further indirectly quench the fluorescence of CDs. In addition, a novel dual-mode detection strategy for As(V) detection based on fluorescence and colorimetry has been presented. Such research has demonstrated that As(V) could adsorb on the surface of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs to alter its surface morphology and manganese valence state, enhance electron transfer from octahedral Mn3O4 NPs to 2,4-DP, and further produce more semiquinone radicals. Hence, more 2,4-DP and 4-AAP would be catalyzed to generate quinone imines, thereby enhancing the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs. Furthermore, such generated quinone imine could quench the fluorescence of CDs. The mechanisms of quinone imine on CDs were confirmed to be IFE and DQE through fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer linear equation, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Hence, a colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode method was constructed for As(V) detection. The method has an LOD of 2.56 μg·L−1 for As(V) in the fluorescence channel and 6.96 μg·L−1 in the colorimetric channel. Such a dual-mode detection method has been successfully applied for As(V) detection in water samples such as tap, lake, and river water. It is encouraging that the detection results of fluorescent and colorimetric channels can be mutually validated, improving the accuracy of the detection method in actual samples. Moreover, the colorimetric signal obtained using the laccase-mimicking activity of octahedral Mn3O4 NPs does not require the participation of H2O2 in the catalytic process, and can avoid the unreliable results caused by various peroxidase existed in the environment. Considering the abovementioned findings, it is believed that such a dual-mode method can provide a facile and rapid alternative to the heavy metal analysis in the environment.
